mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators who need to automate cluster lifecycle management across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the complexity of setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install the necessary components.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This command adds the Cluster API Helm chart repository.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for the Cluster API to function properly, as they define the custom resources used by the operator.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api.crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the CRDs for Cluster API version 1.12.4.

steps.title: Install Cluster API components
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API components into the specified namespace, enabling the operator to manage clusters.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

```

> This command installs Cluster API in the kube-system namespace.

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a custom resource that defines a new cluster to be managed by Cluster API.

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-cluster-infra
EOF

```

> This command creates a new cluster resource named 'my-cluster'.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-cluster-infra
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Cluster API components to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> This command lists all pods in the kube-system namespace to verify that Cluster API components are running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API components
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs. Be aware that this will delete all managed clusters and associated resources, leading to potential data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api.crds.yaml

```

> This command uninstalls the Cluster API Helm release.
> This command removes the CRDs associated with Cluster API.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API to a new version
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your cluster resources. This step includes draining nodes if necessary, upgrading the components, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl get nodes -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> This command drains all nodes in the cluster.
> This command upgrades the Cluster API to version 1.12.4.
> This command verifies that the upgraded pods are running.
commands: kubectl get nodes -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error indicates that the Cluster CRD has not been installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api.crds.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create cluster: infrastructure provider not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when the specified infrastructure provider is not installed or configured.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the infrastructure provider CRDs and components are installed and configured correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in kube-system are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that one or more Cluster API components are failing to start.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the failing pods using: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error typically indicates an issue with the kubeconfig or the API server certificate.

**Fix:**
Verify your kubeconfig file and ensure that the API server certificate is valid.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new infrastructure providers and improved error handling.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your created clusters with their statuses.
codeSnippets:

kind: Cluster
metadata:

  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-cluster-infra
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.26 or higher and have kubectl and Helm installed.
